keywords.rationale: The phrase 'each' signals equal groups, so you multiply the amount in one group by the number of groups.
A repeated amount across many trays means the same group is happening again and again.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which numbers matter when you set up this problem?

options.content: 24 muffins in each tray and 36 trays
24 muffins and 60 muffins because 24 + 36 = 60
36 trays and 12 muffins because 12 is a fact family number

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation should you use first?

options.content: Addition because you are putting two numbers side by side
Multiplication because the same amount is in each tray
Division because you are splitting muffins into trays

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What is the problem really asking you to find?

options.content: How many trays there are
How many muffins fit in one tray
How many muffins there are altogether

keywords.rationale: When a total is shared equally, division tells how much goes in each equal group.
A total split into a fixed number of groups means divide the total by the number of groups.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values should you use in the number sentence?

options.content: 1,386 pencils and 6 equal groups
1,386 pencils and 1,392 because that is the next number
6 groups and 12 pencils because 6 × 2 = 12

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: Which operation matches equal sharing?

options.content: Division because the pencils are shared into equal groups
Multiplication because more groups always means multiply
Subtraction because you take away pencils one at a time

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What result are you trying to find?

options.content: How many pencils are in each group
How many groups there are
How many pencils are left over after one group

keywords.rationale: Each bus holds the same number of students, so divide the total by the size of one group.
You know the total students and the size of each bus group, so division finds the number of buses.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values matter most for solving the bus problem?

options.content: 197 students and 8 students per bus
197 students and 205 students because that is close
8 buses and 197 buses

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: Which operation tells how many buses are needed?

options.content: Division because 197 students are grouped by 8 per bus
Addition because you count up students and buses together
Subtraction because students leave one bus at a time

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What is the problem asking you to decide after dividing?

options.content: Whether the remainder means another bus is needed
Whether 197 is an even number
Whether each bus should have 197 students

keywords.rationale: A fixed bag size means divide the total oranges by 9 to count full bags.
You are counting complete equal groups, so division with the remainder interpreted is needed.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which numbers belong in the division sentence?

options.content: 734 oranges and 9 oranges in each bag
734 oranges and 743 oranges
9 bags and 81 oranges

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation matches full bags of 9 oranges?

options.content: Division because the oranges are grouped into equal bags
Multiplication because more than one bag is used
Addition because oranges are being combined

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What is the answer supposed to represent?

options.content: The number of complete bags that can be filled
The number of oranges in one bag after filling
The total oranges plus the bags

keywords.rationale: A number of identical ticket prices means use multiplication to find the total.
The total cost comes from repeating the same ticket price for each ticket.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values should appear in the equation?

options.content: 9 tickets and a total of 108 dollars
108 tickets and 9 dollars
9 and 117 because 9 + 108 = 117

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation connects ticket price and total cost?

options.content: Multiplication because one ticket price is repeated 9 times
Division because the total should be split first
Subtraction because money is spent

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What unknown does the problem want you to find?

options.content: The cost of one ticket
The number of tickets sold
The number of dollars left over

keywords.rationale: Equal groups across 12 teams means one amount is repeated 12 times.
The total comes from combining the same bottles-per-team amount over all teams.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which numbers matter for the equation?

options.content: 12 teams and 96 total bottles
12 teams and 108 bottles because 12 is close to 96
96 teams and 12 bottles

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: Which operation helps set up the unknown bottles per team?

options.content: Multiplication because bottles per team repeated 12 times equals the total
Addition because 12 + 96 uses all the numbers
Subtraction because bottles are removed from the total

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What unknown are you solving for?

options.content: How many bottles each team gets
How many teams there are
How many bottles are missing

keywords.rationale: When two amounts are combined, addition finds the new total.
Two costs together mean add the decimal amounts.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which money amounts should be used?

options.content: $18.45 and $25.83 because that is the total
$7.38 and $11.07 because those digits look related

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: How should the two amounts be combined?

options.content: Add them because the question asks for the total amount
Subtract them because money problems always compare values
Multiply them because both are decimals

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What is the question asking you to find?

options.content: The total of both money amounts
How much larger the first amount is
How many cents are in one dollar

keywords.rationale: The items must be added together before you can compare them to $10.00.
After finding the total cost, subtraction tells how much money remains.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values belong in the setup?

options.content: $6.75 and $8.64 only

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What sequence of operations should you use?

options.content: Add the item costs, then subtract that total from $10.00
Subtract the item costs from each other, then add $10.00
Multiply the prices because there are two items

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What final answer should represent?

options.content: How much money is left from $10.00
How much the first item costs
How many items were bought

keywords.rationale: Weight is a measurement attribute, so choose the tool that measures weight.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: What information matters most here?

options.content: The attribute is weight and the object is a watermelon
The object is round so use a ruler
The object is cold so use a thermometer

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: Which skill is the question using?

options.content: Measurement because you must match an attribute to the correct tool
Addition because tools and objects go together
Division because watermelons can be cut into parts

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What answer should you give?

options.content: The tool that measures weight
The color of the watermelon
The temperature of the watermelon

keywords.rationale: A unit conversion question uses the given measurement and the unit relationship.
Because 1 yard equals 3 feet, multiply the number of yards by 3.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values are needed for the conversion?

options.content: 4 yards and the fact that 1 yard = 3 feet
4 feet and 3 yards
4 and 7 because 4 + 3 = 7

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: How do you convert the rope length?

options.content: Multiply because each yard contains 3 feet
Subtract because feet are smaller than yards
Divide because you are changing units

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What should the final answer tell?

options.content: How many feet the rope measures
How many yards fit in one foot
How many ropes there are

keywords.rationale: The problem gives a measurement amount that must be converted to a smaller unit.
Since 1 quart equals 4 cups, multiply the number of quarts by 4.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values should guide the conversion?

options.content: 3 quarts and the fact that 1 quart = 4 cups
3 cups and 4 quarts
3 and 7 because 3 + 4 = 7

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation converts the quarts into cups?

options.content: Multiplication because each quart contains 4 cups
Subtraction because cups are smaller units
Division because the word 'how many' always means divide

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What should the answer describe?

options.content: How many cups the pitcher holds
How many quarts are left over
How many pitchers are needed

keywords.rationale: When a trip happens later in time, add the elapsed time to the start time.
To find the arrival time, move forward from the departure time by the elapsed time.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which time values matter in this problem?

options.content: 8:35 a.m., 1 hour, and 45 minutes
8:35 and 8:45 because both show 8
1:45 and 10:20 because they look like clock times

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What should you do with the elapsed time?

options.content: Add it to 8:35 because the bus arrives later
Subtract it because time passes away
Multiply the hour and minutes

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What result are you finding?

options.content: The arrival time of the bus
The number of buses leaving
The length of one hour

keywords.rationale: A daily distance repeated for 5 days means multiply the one-day total by 5.

keywords.phrase: to the park and then back home
keywords.rationale: The two one-way distances must be added to find the daily total first.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values matter when setting up the distance problem?

options.content: 3/4 mile, 3/4 mile, and 5 days
3/4 mile and 10 days
5 miles and 3/4 day

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation plan matches the story?

options.content: Add the two trips for one day, then multiply by 5 days
Subtract the return trip from the first trip, then multiply
Divide 5 by 3/4 because there are five days

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What should the final answer represent?

options.content: The total miles Lena walks over all 5 days
The miles in one direction only
The number of times Lena walks to the park

keywords.rationale: The problem is asking for two measurement formulas using the same rectangle dimensions.
Area uses multiplication of length and width, while perimeter uses addition around the shape.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values belong in the formulas?

options.content: Length 14 and width 6
Area 20 and perimeter 84
14, 6, and 20 because 14 + 6 = 20

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation plan matches the two measurements?

options.content: Multiply for area and add all four sides for perimeter
Add for area and multiply for perimeter
Subtract width from length for both answers

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What is the problem asking you to report?

options.content: Both the area and the perimeter of the rectangle
Only the perimeter
Only the area

keywords.rationale: If area = length × width, then length = area ÷ width.
You know the total area and one side, so divide to find the unknown side.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values help find the missing side?

options.content: Area 72 and width 8
Area 72 and length 9
Width 8 and perimeter 80

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What operation finds the missing length?

options.content: Division because 72 square units are split into rows of 8
Addition because you need a larger side
Subtraction because one side is unknown

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What should the answer tell you?

options.content: The missing length of the rectangle
The total area of two rectangles
The width written a second time

keywords.rationale: You must compare the listed distances to the 1-mile benchmark first.
A part out of a whole is represented as a fraction or decimal of the total.

guidedSteps.title: Important values
guidedSteps.prompt: Which values drive the fraction you need?

options.content: The 3 hikers below 1 mile and the 10 hikers total
The 1 mile benchmark and 13 hikers
Only the total of 10 hikers

guidedSteps.title: Operation
guidedSteps.prompt: What math idea best matches this question?

options.content: Form a part-to-whole fraction after counting who is below 1 mile
Add every hiker's distance together
Multiply 3 by 10 because both numbers appear

guidedSteps.title: Main question
guidedSteps.prompt: What does the final answer represent?

options.content: The fraction of hikers whose distance is below 1 mile
The total number of miles walked by all hikers
The number of hikers who walked exactly 1 mile
